seo.title: Education AI Coding Assistant AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai coding assistant AI agents in education. Covers FERPA, COPPA, State education data privacy laws.

content.intro: Deploying an AI coding assistant in educational settings introduces unique compliance obligations under FERPA, COPPA, and state-level student data privacy laws, requiring careful governance of how student code, identity, and learning data are handled. Unlike general-purpose coding tools, educational AI agents must balance pedagogical integrity—ensuring AI augments rather than replaces qualified instruction—with strict content safety standards for minors. This checklist provides concrete, actionable controls to protect students, satisfy regulators, and maintain institutional trust.

sections.heading: Student Data Privacy & Consent
sections.description: Ensure all student data collection, storage, and processing complies with FERPA, COPPA, and applicable state laws before the agent handles any student-identifiable information.

items.label: Obtain verifiable parental consent for users under 13
items.description: Implement a COPPA-compliant consent workflow that collects verifiable parental or guardian consent before any student under 13 can interact with the AI coding assistant or have their data stored. Document consent records with timestamps and guardian identity verification.

items.label: Classify and tag all student-generated data as FERPA-protected
items.description: Automatically classify code submissions, debugging sessions, test results, and chat logs as education records under FERPA. Apply data classification tags at ingestion so downstream systems enforce appropriate access controls.

items.label: Restrict data sharing to school officials with legitimate educational interest
items.description: Configure role-based access controls so that student coding data is accessible only to the assigned instructor, school IT administrators, and the student themselves. Block third-party LLM provider training use of student data via contractual Data Processing Agreements.

items.label: Execute a signed Student Data Privacy Agreement with the LLM vendor
items.description: Before go-live, obtain a signed agreement from the AI model provider explicitly prohibiting use of student data for model training, requiring breach notification within 72 hours, and specifying data deletion timelines upon contract termination.

items.label: Implement data minimization: collect only what is pedagogically necessary
items.description: Audit every data field collected by the agent (e.g., keystrokes, session duration, repository metadata) and remove or anonymize fields not directly required for coding assistance or grade reporting. Document the legal basis for each retained field.

items.label: Provide students and parents a data access and deletion request mechanism
items.description: Build a self-service portal or documented process allowing students (or parents of minors) to request access to, correction of, or deletion of their education records held by the AI system within 45 days, consistent with FERPA requirements.

items.label: Log all data access events to a tamper-evident audit trail
items.description: Record every access to student coding data—including AI agent reads of repository context—in an immutable audit log with user identity, timestamp, and purpose. Retain logs for a minimum of 5 years to support FERPA compliance audits.

sections.heading: Content Safety & Pedagogical Integrity
sections.description: Ensure the AI coding assistant produces age-appropriate, bias-free content and operates as a learning aid that supports—not supplants—qualified instructor oversight.

items.label: Deploy age-appropriate content filters calibrated to grade level
items.description: Configure output filters that block profanity, adult content, and off-topic material inappropriate for the enrolled student's grade band (K-8, 9-12, higher ed). Test filters quarterly with adversarial prompts targeting each age group.

items.label: Enforce a 'Socratic mode' that guides rather than directly solves assignments
items.description: Implement a pedagogical guardrail that detects when a student submits an assignment prompt verbatim and responds with guiding questions or hints instead of complete solutions, preventing academic dishonesty and preserving learning outcomes.

items.label: Require instructor review before AI-generated code is submitted as coursework
items.description: Integrate a workflow flag that marks AI-assisted code segments and routes them through an instructor approval step before the student can submit to a grading system, ensuring qualified human oversight of AI contributions.

items.label: Run bias audits on AI responses across demographic groups quarterly
items.description: Use a representative test set of student personas (varying gender, ethnicity, disability status, English proficiency) to evaluate whether the AI coding assistant provides equitable quality of feedback and explanations. Document findings and remediation actions.

items.label: Block the agent from providing mental health, legal, or medical advice
items.description: Implement topic classifiers that detect out-of-scope queries (e.g., student distress signals, requests for legal or medical guidance) and redirect students to appropriate school counseling or support resources rather than generating AI responses.

items.label: Validate that all code examples in responses are free of harmful or malicious patterns
items.description: Before returning any code snippet to a student, pass it through a static analysis tool that flags malware patterns, exploit code, or instructions for bypassing security controls, and suppress such content with an explanation.

items.label: Maintain a human escalation path for contested AI feedback
items.description: Provide a clearly labeled 'Dispute AI Feedback' button in the UI that routes the student's session transcript to their instructor for human review within one business day, ensuring qualified instructors remain the authoritative source of academic judgment.

sections.heading: Code Security & Technical Verification
sections.description: Apply rigorous automated verification to all AI-generated and student-submitted code to prevent security vulnerabilities, credential leakage, unsafe dependencies, and license violations from entering student repositories.

items.label: Scan all AI-generated code for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities before delivery
items.description: Integrate a SAST tool (e.g., Semgrep, CodeQL) into the agent's output pipeline to scan every code suggestion for SQL injection, XSS, insecure deserialization, and other OWASP Top 10 patterns before the response is shown to the student.

items.label: Detect and redact hardcoded credentials, API keys, and secrets in all code
items.description: Run a secrets detection tool (e.g., Gitleaks, TruffleHog) on both student-submitted repository context ingested by the agent and all AI-generated code outputs. Automatically redact detected secrets and alert the student and instructor.

items.label: Validate sandbox execution is fully isolated from production networks and student PII
items.description: Confirm that the code execution sandbox has no network egress to external services, no access to student identity stores, and runs in an ephemeral container that is destroyed after each session. Conduct network isolation tests before each semester deployment.

items.label: Audit all suggested third-party dependencies against a known-vulnerability database
items.description: Before recommending any npm, pip, Maven, or other package, query the NVD or OSV database for known CVEs with CVSS score ≥ 7.0. Block suggestions of packages with critical vulnerabilities and surface safer alternatives to the student.

items.label: Enforce open-source license compatibility checks on all suggested dependencies
items.description: Integrate a license compliance scanner (e.g., FOSSA, License Finder) that flags dependencies with GPL, AGPL, or other copyleft licenses incompatible with the institution's acceptable use policy, and presents the student with license metadata before adoption.

items.label: Validate AI-suggested test cases achieve minimum coverage thresholds before acceptance
items.description: Require that AI-generated unit tests achieve at least 80% line coverage on the target function before they are accepted into the student's repository. Surface coverage reports to the student as a learning artifact.

items.label: Implement iterative debugging loop limits to prevent runaway agent execution
items.description: Cap the AI agent's autonomous debugging iterations at a configurable maximum (default: 5 cycles) per session to prevent infinite loops, excessive compute costs, and unintended code mutations. Log each iteration for instructor review.

items.label: Verify repository context ingestion does not pull files outside the student's assigned scope
items.description: Enforce path-based access controls on the repository context window so the agent can only read files within the student's assigned project directory, preventing cross-student data leakage in shared repository environments.

sections.heading: Accessibility, Governance & Incident Response
sections.description: Ensure the AI coding assistant meets Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards, establishes clear governance accountability, and has a tested incident response plan for data breaches and harmful content events.

items.label: Conduct Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility audit before launch
items.description: Engage an independent accessibility auditor to test the AI coding assistant UI against Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA criteria, including screen reader compatibility, keyboard navigation, color contrast ratios, and alternative text for code visualizations. Remediate all critical findings before student access.

items.label: Designate a named FERPA Compliance Officer responsible for AI data governance
items.description: Assign a specific individual (not just a team) as the FERPA Compliance Officer for the AI coding assistant deployment. Document their responsibilities, escalation authority, and contact information in the system's governance charter.

items.label: Develop and test a student data breach response plan specific to AI system incidents
items.description: Create a written incident response playbook covering: detection of unauthorized AI access to student records, notification of affected students and parents within 72 hours, regulatory reporting to the Department of Education, and evidence preservation procedures. Conduct a tabletop exercise before go-live.

items.label: Publish a plain-language AI use disclosure in the student and parent handbook
items.description: Add a dedicated section to the student/parent handbook explaining what the AI coding assistant does, what data it collects, how long data is retained, and how to opt out. Make this disclosure available in the top 3 languages spoken by the student population.

items.label: Conduct annual third-party penetration testing of the AI agent infrastructure
items.description: Commission an annual penetration test by a qualified third party covering the LLM API integration, sandbox execution environment, student authentication flows, and repository access controls. Track and remediate all high and critical findings within 30 days.

items.label: Establish a student feedback channel for reporting harmful or incorrect AI outputs
items.description: Implement a one-click 'Report a Problem' button in the coding assistant UI that captures the session context and routes the report to both the instructor and the AI governance team within 24 hours. Track report volume and resolution time as a KPI.

items.label: Review and update AI system risk assessment at the start of each academic year
items.description: Schedule an annual review of the AI coding assistant's risk assessment to incorporate new state education data privacy laws, updated COPPA guidance, changes to the LLM model version, and lessons learned from the prior year's incidents and audits.

notes.note: All student coding submissions, AI interaction logs, and debugging session transcripts constitute 'education records' under FERPA. The institution must ensure the LLM vendor is designated as a 'school official' with a legitimate educational interest via a written agreement, and that the vendor does not re-disclose student data for any purpose—including model training—without explicit consent. Violations can result in loss of federal funding.

If the AI coding assistant is accessible to students under 13, the operator must obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting any personal information, including IP addresses, usage logs, or code content. Schools acting as intermediaries under the 'school official' exception must provide parents with direct notice and ensure the AI tool is used solely for educational purposes with no commercial data use.

Over 45 states have enacted student data privacy laws (e.g., California's SOPIPA, New York's Education Law 2-d, Texas's SCOPE Act) that impose requirements beyond FERPA, including prohibitions on targeted advertising, mandatory data security standards, and breach notification timelines as short as 30 days. Institutions must map their deployment state's specific requirements and include them in vendor contracts before launch.

notes.regulation: Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act & WCAG 2.1
notes.note: Any AI coding assistant deployed by a federally funded educational institution must conform to Section 508 standards, which incorporate WCAG 2.1 Level AA criteria. This includes ensuring the code editor interface, AI chat panel, and output visualizations are fully operable by students using assistive technologies such as screen readers and switch access devices. Non-compliance exposes the institution to OCR complaints and potential loss of federal funding.

cta.heading: Automate Your Education Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI coding assistant against FERPA, COPPA, and state education data privacy requirements—so your institution stays compliant as regulations evolve and student data stays protected.
